executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of the public health emergency unwinding period. Year-over-year decreases ranged from modest reductions of 1.8% in New York to more significant drops of 7.6% in Michigan, while North Carolina was the sole major state showing growth at 0.5%. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, experienced a 3.1% decline, positioning it in the middle range of enrollment changes among large states. Total national Medicaid expenditures for fiscal year 2024 reached $215.9 billion, with a national per-enrollee average of $2,869. Spending patterns reveal substantial state variation, with per-enrollee costs ranging from Florida's $2,031 to Pennsylvania's $4,377. Texas maintains relatively controlled spending at $2,988 per enrollee, approximately 4% above the national average, while managing the fourth-largest state program with $12.0 billion in total expenditures.

Quality performance metrics show significant room for improvement across the Medicaid system, with a national average quality score of 65.6 out of 100 points. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below the national average. This performance gap is particularly notable given Texas's high managed care penetration rate of 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, suggesting that the state's delivery system structure should theoretically support better quality outcomes. The quality performance disparity between Texas and leading states like Massachusetts (81 points) represents a critical opportunity for targeted interventions. Recent federal regulatory developments, including proposed 2028 HCBS quality measures and updated data collection requirements, signal increased federal oversight that will likely require enhanced state quality improvement initiatives and reporting capabilities.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving only 8.8% of the U.S. population. The state's enrollment decline of 3.1% year-over-year aligns closely with national unwinding trends and mirrors peer state Pennsylvania exactly, while performing better than large northern states like Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). However, Texas significantly underperforms compared to fellow non-expansion southern state North Carolina, which achieved positive growth (+0.5%). At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends 4% above the national average of $2,869, positioning it in the middle tier among large states—higher than Florida ($2,031) and North Carolina ($2,616) but substantially below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561). This spending pattern reflects the constraints of serving a narrower eligibility population while managing complex urban and rural healthcare needs across the nation's second-largest state.

Texas demonstrates operational strength through its robust managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% penetration rates significantly exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating sophisticated care coordination capabilities that should yield long-term cost efficiencies. However, the state's quality performance presents a critical challenge, scoring 62/100 and ranking 30th nationally—trailing most peer large states and clustering with lower-performing southern non-expansion states rather than leading programs. This quality gap, combined with Texas's historical resistance to Medicaid expansion, creates a concerning trajectory where the state manages substantial enrollment and expenditures without commensurate health outcomes. Key areas to monitor include whether the state's managed care investments translate into measurable quality improvements, how enrollment stabilizes post-unwinding given Texas's large uninsured population, and whether federal regulatory changes around HCBS quality measures and data collection requirements will pressure Texas to enhance its quality infrastructure to maintain federal funding stability.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Florida Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating cost efficiency or possible underinvestment in care.

alerts.title: Southern States Dominate Bottom Quality Rankings
alerts.description: Four of the bottom five quality performers are Southern states with scores below 55, raising concerns about regional care quality disparities.

alerts.title: Massachusetts High Spending With Top Quality Performance
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines the highest per-enrollee spending ($4,201) with the top quality score (81), suggesting effective high-investment care model.

alerts.title: Texas Above-Average Spending Despite Moderate Quality
alerts.description: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee (above national average) but ranks only 30th in quality scores, indicating potential efficiency concerns.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk tier - ranks #30 nationally at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality performance sits at 62/100, ranking 30th nationally and placing it in the moderate tier, just 2 points above the low performer threshold of 60. This puts Texas significantly below the national average of 65.6.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals, federal funding, and regulatory scrutiny. Being close to low performer status creates vulnerability for HHSC and potential contract risk for Accenture.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs $120 above national average despite efficiency
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,868.64, representing a $120 premium. With over 4 million enrollees, this translates to approximately $484 million in excess spending annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs signal potential inefficiencies or service delivery issues that HHSC needs to address. This creates opportunities for Accenture to propose cost optimization initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost benchmarking analysis with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration leads nation at 93.3% vs 83.2% average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86 million enrollees, significantly outperforming the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong operational execution in managed care transition.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration demonstrates Texas's operational sophistication and creates leverage for Accenture to showcase successful implementation capabilities to other state prospects.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Document Texas managed care best practices for replication opportunities

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 create compliance requirements
executiveInsights.summary: CMS has announced new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028, creating upcoming regulatory compliance requirements. This appears in recent Federal Register postings from April 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates, reporting changes, and operational adjustments that HHSC needs to prepare for. This creates potential scope expansion opportunities for Accenture's contract.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements with compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment decline of 3.1% matches national unwinding trend
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year to 4.03 million, aligning with the broader national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. Most large states show similar 3-7% declines.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment decline impacts revenue projections and system capacity planning, but Texas's decline rate suggests controlled unwinding execution rather than operational problems.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment forecasting models with HHSC planning team

riskOpportunity.label: Quality Score Improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for improvement initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting top performance gaps.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment Decline Management
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, requiring systems adjustments and cost optimization.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement dynamic capacity planning tools to manage enrollment fluctuations efficiently.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS Quality Measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set requires system updates.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Lead early adoption planning for HCBS quality measurement requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: HHS OIG Oversight
riskOpportunity.description: Active OIG oversight docket presents compliance risks but also opportunities for remediation leadership.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish proactive OIG compliance monitoring and rapid response capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed Care Optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas has 93.3% MC penetration above national average, indicating mature market for optimization services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop advanced analytics solutions for mature managed care market optimization.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC Procurement Pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement opportunities present competitive bidding landscape.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated HHSC procurement intelligence and response team.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-Enrollee Cost Efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee above national average, indicating potential efficiency improvement needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost reduction strategies targeting the $120 per-enrollee gap above national average.

riskOpportunity.label: CHIP Data Collection
riskOpportunity.description: New Medicaid and CHIP generic information collection activities create compliance and system requirements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position for early engagement on CHIP data collection system modernization needs.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spent $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee in fiscal year 2024, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees, well above the 83.2% national average.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in the moderate performance tier.
